description: UE5 subsystem framework: Engine, Editor, GameInstance, World, and LocalPlayer subsystems for clean singleton-like architecture

entries.title: Subsystems Overview
entries.description: Subsystems are auto-instanced UObjects tied to a specific engine lifetime (Engine, Editor, GameInstance, World, LocalPlayer). They provide a clean alternative to singletons for game-wide systems.
entries.whatItIs: Subsystems are UObject classes that the engine automatically creates and destroys based on a specific lifetime scope. You simply subclass one of the five subsystem base classes and the engine handles instantiation, lifecycle, and access — no manual registration or singleton boilerplate required.
whenToUse: Building game-wide service systems (quest manager, inventory manager, audio manager) that need a well-defined lifetime
When you need a singleton-like pattern but want automatic lifecycle management tied to engine scopes
Per-world systems that should be created fresh for each level (spawn managers, weather systems)
Per-player systems in splitscreen or multiplayer (input mapping, per-player UI state)
whenNotToUse: When the system needs to be an Actor in the world with transforms, components, or replication — use an Actor or GameState instead
For tiny utility functions that do not need persistent state — a static function library (UBlueprintFunctionLibrary) is simpler
When you need multiple instances of the same system type — subsystems are one-per-scope by design
Editor-only tools in a packaged game — UEditorSubsystem does not exist in shipping builds
commonMistakes: Using a UWorldSubsystem for data that must survive level transitions — it gets destroyed on level change. Use UGameInstanceSubsystem instead.
Forgetting that subsystems are UObjects, not Actors — they cannot replicate, tick (unless UTickableWorldSubsystem), or have components
Trying to access a World subsystem during GameInstance initialization — the World may not exist yet. Use ShouldCreateSubsystem() and check dependencies.
Assuming subsystems exist in all contexts — UEditorSubsystem is not available in packaged builds, and ULocalPlayerSubsystem requires a valid LocalPlayer
Not calling Super::Initialize() or Super::Deinitialize() when overriding lifecycle methods
keyPoints: UEngineSubsystem: lives as long as the engine (GEngine). Survives everything.
UEditorSubsystem: lives as long as the editor. Not available in packaged builds.
UGameInstanceSubsystem: lives as long as the GameInstance. Survives level loads.
UWorldSubsystem: lives as long as the UWorld. One per world, destroyed on level change.
ULocalPlayerSubsystem: one per local player. Useful for per-player systems in splitscreen.
Auto-created: just subclass — no manual registration. Engine finds and creates them.
Override Initialize() and Deinitialize() for setup/teardown.
